---
title: "Projections & planets"
---

Module: `oblimap_projection_module` (`src/projection_oblimap2.f90`), ported from
[OBLIMAP2](https://github.com/iceage-group/oblimap) (Reerink et al., 2010,
*Geosci. Model Dev.*). Projections convert between geographic (`lon`, `lat`)
and projected Cartesian (`x`, `y`) coordinates.

## Supported `mtype` values

| `mtype` string | Projected? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `latitude_longitude` / `latlon` / `gaussian` | no | geographic; `x,y` ≡ `lon,lat` |
| `cartesian` | no (generic) | plain planar coordinates, Euclidean distance |
| `stereographic` / `polar_stereographic` | yes | oblique & polar stereographic |
| `lambert_azimuthal_equal_area` | yes | oblique Lambert azimuthal equal-area |

Projected types are realised in both **spherical** and **ellipsoidal (Snyder)**
forms, selected automatically from the chosen [planet](#planets):

| Family | Sphere method | Ellipsoid method |
|---|---|---|
| Stereographic (oblique) | `oblique_sg_projection` | `oblique_sg_projection_ellipsoid_snyder` |
| Stereographic (polar special case) | — | `polar_sg_projection_ellipsoid_snyder` |
| Lambert azimuthal equal-area | `oblique_laea_projection_snyder` | `oblique_laea_projection_ellipsoid_snyder` |

## Projection parameters

Stored in `projection_class`; set via `grid_init` / `points_init` arguments of
the same name. All angles in degrees.

| Parameter | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `lambda` | longitude of the projection centre (central meridian) |
| `phi` | latitude of the projection centre (standard parallel) |
| `alpha` | oblique tangent angle — where the projection plane slices the sphere |
| `x_e` | false easting (x offset) |
| `y_n` | false northing (y offset) |

`alpha` controls the plane's intersection with the spherical surface: the
smaller the domain, the smaller the `alpha` that minimises distortion. A helper
`optimal_alpha(R, nx, ny, dx, dy)` estimates a good value for given grid
dimensions.

### Typical regional setups (Reerink et al., 2010)

| Domain | `lambda` | `phi` | `alpha` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Greenland | 320° (= −40°) | 72° | 7.5° |
| Antarctica | 0° | −90° | 19.0° |

```fortran
call grid_init(gGRL, name="GRL-20KM", mtype="stereographic", units="kilometers", &
               dx=20.d0, nx=76, dy=20.d0, ny=151, &
               lambda=-40.d0, phi=72.d0, alpha=7.5d0)
```

## Forward & inverse transforms

Dispatched by the projection's `method`:

| Routine | Direction |
|---|---|
| `oblimap_projection(lambda, phi, x_out, y_out, proj)` | lon/lat → x/y |
| `oblimap_projection_inverse(x, y, lambda_out, phi_out, proj)` | x/y → lon/lat |

Supporting routines include `projection_init`, `same_projection` (equivalence
test, used by `map_init` to decide whether two domains share a system),
`optimal_alpha`, and the rotation helpers `rotation_projection` /
`inverse_rotation_projection`.

## Planets

Module: `planet` (`src/planet.f90`). A `planet_class` carries the semi-major
axis `a`, eccentricity `e`, flattening `f`, mean radius `R`, and an `is_sphere`
flag. Two are predefined:

| `name` | Shape | Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| `"WGS84"` | ellipsoid | `a = 6 378 137 m`, `f = 1/298.257223563`, `e = 0.0818…`, `R ≈ 6.37122×10⁶ m` |
| `"Spherical Earth"` | sphere | `R ≈ 6.37122×10⁶ m`, `f ≈ 0`, `e = 0` |

`WGS84` is the default. Geodesic distances and polygon areas on the ellipsoid
are computed with Karney's GeographicLib (`geodesic.f90`); the spherical planet
uses great-circle formulas.

### Distance & area routines

| Routine | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `planet_distance(a, f, lon1, lat1, lon2, lat2)` | geodesic distance |
| `spherical_distance(...)` | great-circle distance |
| `cartesian_distance(x1, y1, x2, y2)` | Euclidean distance |
| `planet_area(a, f, lons, lats)` | geodesic polygon area |
| `cartesian_area(x, y)` | planar polygon area (shoelace) |

::: {.callout-tip}
## Extensibility
Adding a planet means extending `planet_init`; adding a projection means adding
forward/inverse routines and a `method` branch in `projection_init` /
`oblimap_projection`. Broadening projection support (e.g. Lambert conformal,
Mercator, rotated pole) is noted as a generalisation opportunity in the
[improvement proposal](../improvements.qmd).
:::
